name: Quadrantids
description: One of the strongest showers of the year, but with a very brief peak of only a few hours. The meteors appear to radiate from near the end of the Big Dipper's handle.

name: Lyrids
description: One of the oldest recorded meteor showers, observed for at least 2,700 years. Bright meteors that sometimes leave glowing dust trails lasting several seconds.
bestViewing: After midnight, looking east toward Lyra

name: Eta Aquariids
description: Debris from Halley's Comet, one of the most famous comets in history. The meteors are fast and often leave persistent glowing trails. Best seen from the southern hemisphere.
bestViewing: Just before dawn, low in the southeast

name: Delta Aquariids
description: A steady, reliable summer shower best seen from tropical latitudes. The meteors tend to be faint but numerous, and the shower overlaps nicely with the Perseids.
bestViewing: After midnight, looking south toward Aquarius

name: Perseids
description: The most beloved shower of the year. Swift, bright meteors from Comet Swift-Tuttle, which last passed through the inner solar system in 1992 and won't return until 2125. Worth losing sleep over.
bestViewing: After midnight, looking northeast toward Perseus

name: Orionids
description: Another gift from Halley's Comet, these fast meteors leave long persistent trails. The shower peaks when the radiant point in Orion is high in the sky around 2am.

name: Leonids
description: Famous for producing legendary meteor storms roughly every 33 years when Earth passes through dense debris clouds. Most years are modest, but every Leonid is fast and brilliant.
bestViewing: After midnight, looking east toward Leo

name: Geminids
description: The king of meteor showers, and the only major shower produced by an asteroid rather than a comet. Bright, multicolored meteors appear from nightfall onward, making it uniquely accessible even for early risers.
bestViewing: All night, radiant highest around midnight

name: Ursids
description: A quiet shower from near the Little Dipper, active during the longest nights of the year. Not spectacular in numbers, but the few meteors you see are often bright and slow-moving.
